message.content:
You are a senior applied cryptographer doing a DEEP second-pass audit of Coinbase cb-mpc. A first broad pass found nothing; go deeper and more exhaustive on this narrow surface. Rigor over volume.
REPO ROOT: C:/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc. Read the named files IN FULL; follow into include/cbmpc/** and include-internal/** for definitions. Anchor every finding to a public API entry (ecdsa_mp.h).

BOUNTY SCOPE: eligible only if reachable via public APIs in include/cbmpc/api/. demo-* and include/cbmpc/c_api/* are OUT OF SCOPE as entry points. Primitive/ZK bugs eligible only when tied to a named public-API path. Medium+ needs an independent-parties PoC (>=1 honest party on unmodified code; malicious party interacts only through the public protocol boundary). Tiers: Critical $50k (key compromise/RCE), High $15k, Medium $2k, Low $200.

SURFACE: Multi-party ECDSA signing rounds. Read src/cbmpc/protocol/ecdsa_mp.cpp signing path IN FULL. Enumerate every received value per signing round (nonce commitments, MtA messages/ciphertexts, partial signatures, ZK/range proofs). Check nonce-share handling for bias/reuse, MtA range-proof verification, consistency checks tying partial sigs to committed shares. Hunt for a malicious signer recovering an honest party share or biasing/forging.

Build an explicit mental table: received-value -> is it validated? where? if not, attack. Report ONLY values used without a required check that a malicious party can exploit with honest counterparties on unmodified code.
At most 5 findings, best-first; ZERO is a fully acceptable, honest result. Each finding: public_api_entry, exact file:line, the precise missing_check, data_flow with function names, attacker_role, impact, poc_sketch through the public API, honest confidence in [0,1].
In coverage_notes, briefly list the received values you DID confirm are properly validated (evidence the pass was thorough). Return ONLY the structured object.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	#pragma once
2	
3	#include <cbmpc/api/curve.h>
4	#include <cbmpc/core/access_structure.h>
5	#include <cbmpc/core/buf.h>
6	#include <cbmpc/core/error.h>
7	#include <cbmpc/core/job.h>
8	
9	// All the functions have two versions: additive and ac. Additive means that the
10	// sharing is additive, while ac means that the sharing is according to a given access structure.
11	namespace coinbase::api::ecdsa_mp {
12	
13	// Run the multi-party key generation protocol.
14	//
15	// The output `key_blob` is a versioned, opaque byte string that can be persisted
16	// by the caller and used in other API calls.
17	//
18	// Supported curves: `curve_id::p256`, `curve_id::secp256k1`.
19	error_t dkg_additive(const job_mp_t& job, curve_id curve, buf_t& key_blob, buf_t& sid);
20	
21	// Run the multi-party key generation protocol with a general access
22	// structure.
23	//
24	// Notes:
25	// - This is an n-party protocol: **all** parties in
26	//   `job.party_names` must be online and participate.
27	// - Only the provided `quorum_party_names` actively contribute to the generated
28	//   key shares.
29	// - The output key blob represents an access-structure key share and
30	//   is not directly usable with `sign_additive()`. Use `sign_ac()` to sign with an online
31	//   quorum (it derives additive shares internally).
32	// - `sid` is an in/out session id used by the protocol. Callers may pass an
33	//   empty buffer to let the protocol derive one.
34	//
35	// Supported curves: `curve_id::p256`, `curve_id::secp256k1`.
36	error_t dkg_ac(const job_mp_t& job, curve_id curve, buf_t& sid, const access_structure_t& access_structure,
37	               const std::vector<std::string_view>& quorum_party_names, buf_t& key_blob);
38	
39	// Refresh an existing key share set, producing a new key share.
40	//
41	// `sid` is an in/out session id used by the refresh protocol. Callers may pass
42	// an empty buffer to let the protocol derive one.
43	error_t refresh_additive(const job_mp_t& job, buf_t& sid, mem_t key_blob, buf_t& new_key_blob);
44	
45	// Refresh an existing key share set using the access-structure refresh protocol.
46	//
47	// Notes:
48	// - See `dkg_ac` for protocol participation semantics.
49	// - The output key blob represents an access-structure key share and
50	//   is not directly usable with `sign_additive()`. Use `sign_ac()` to sign with an online
51	//   quorum (it derives additive shares internally).
52	// - `sid` is an in/out session id used by the protocol. Callers may pass an
53	//   empty buffer to let the protocol derive one.
54	error_t refresh_ac(const job_mp_t& job, buf_t& sid, mem_t key_blob, const access_structure_t& access_structure,
55	                   const std::vector<std::string_view>& quorum_party_names, buf_t& new_key_blob);
56	
57	// Sign a message with ECDSA-MP and output signature on `sig_receiver`.
58	//
59	// Output signature is DER encoded.
60	//
61	// Note: the underlying protocol returns the signature only on `sig_receiver`. On
62	// other parties, `sig_der` may be left empty on success.
63	error_t sign_additive(const job_mp_t& job, mem_t key_blob, mem_t msg, party_idx_t sig_receiver, buf_t& sig_der);
64	
65	// Sign a message with ECDSA-MP using an access-structure key share (from
66	// `dkg_ac` / `refresh_ac`).
67	//
68	// This API first derives an additive-share signing key for the **online** signing
69	// parties in `job.party_names` and then runs the normal `sign_additive()` protocol among
70	// those parties.
71	//
72	// Notes:
73	// - Unlike `dkg_ac` / `refresh_ac`, `sign_ac` only requires the parties in
74	//   `job.party_names` to be online and participate.
75	// - Output semantics match `sign_additive()`: the signature is returned only on
76	//   `sig_receiver`. On other parties, `sig_der` may be left empty on success.
77	error_t sign_ac(const job_mp_t& job, mem_t ac_key_blob, const access_structure_t& access_structure, mem_t msg,
78	                party_idx_t sig_receiver, buf_t& sig_der);
79	
80	// Get the compressed public key from a key blob (SEC1 compressed point).
81	//
82	// This is the same encoding as `ecdsa_2p::get_public_key_compressed`.
83	error_t get_public_key_compressed(mem_t key_blob, buf_t& pub_key);
84	
85	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
86	// Key blob manipulation (private share backup / restore)
87	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
88	
89	// Get this party's share public point (Qi) from a key blob, returning SEC1
90	// compressed point encoding.
91	//
92	// This is useful for verifiable backup schemes like PVE, where a scalar x can be
93	// verified against its corresponding curve point Q = x*G.
94	//
95	// Notes:
96	// - Works for both additive (v1) and access-structure (v2) ECDSA-MP key blobs.
97	// - This can be called on a blob produced by `detach_private_scalar` since Qi_self
98	//   remains present in the key blob even after redaction.
99	error_t get_public_share_compressed(mem_t key_blob, buf_t& out_public_share_compressed);
100	
101	// Detach the private scalar share from a key blob, producing:
102	// - a "public" key blob with its private scalar wiped, and
103	// - the private scalar x encoded as a fixed-length big-endian buffer.
104	//
105	// The public blob is safe to persist as public-only material, but is not usable
106	// for signing/refresh until restored with `attach_private_scalar`.
107	//
108	// Output:
109	// - `out_private_scalar_fixed` length equals the curve order size in bytes
110	//   (e.g., 32 bytes for secp256k1/p256).
111	error_t detach_private_scalar(mem_t key_blob, buf_t& out_public_key_blob, buf_t& out_private_scalar_fixed);
112	
113	// Restore a full key blob by attaching a fixed-length private scalar x into a
114	// public key blob (produced by `detach_private_scalar`).
115	//
116	// This validates that x matches the key blob by checking:
117	// - `public_share_compressed` matches the blob's Qi_self, and
118	// - x*G == Qi_self.
119	//
120	// Input:
121	// - `private_scalar_fixed` must be a fixed-length big-endian scalar encoding with
122	//   length equal to the curve order size in bytes.
123	// - `public_share_compressed` must be the SEC1 compressed point encoding of
124	//   this party's share public point (Qi_self), e.g. from `get_public_share_compressed`.
125	error_t attach_private_scalar(mem_t public_key_blob, mem_t private_scalar_fixed, mem_t public_share_compressed,
126	                              buf_t& out_key_blob);
127	
128	}  // namespace coinbase::api::ecdsa_mp
129

content.text:
This is a thorough protocol. Let me examine the critical final round closely. The signature output uses `rho_k._j` and `beta._j` received in round 9 (line 461). Let me check what consistency ties these to the verified commitments.

Key observation: At line 461, parties send `rho_k` (cleartext rho_k_i) and `beta` (cleartext) to sig_receiver, along with proofs `pi_R_eRHO_K` and `pi_R_eB`. The sig_receiver verifies these against `eRHO_K._j` and `eB[j]`. Let me verify the proof binds the cleartext value to the commitment. Let me read the relevant ZK proof and the final output verification carefully.

The final-round proof `pi_R_eB` proves `B = beta*G` is the opening of commitment `eB[j]` (the recombined `r*eRHO_X + m*eRHO_i`). The receiver verifies `B.L = beta*G` via `elgamal_com_pub_share_equ`. This binds `beta._j` to `eB[j]`. Similarly `rho_k._j` is bound to `eRHO_K._j`. So the final values are pinned to verified commitments. This path looks solid.

Now the crucial nonce-related question: at line 478-479, the signature `s` is computed and then verified against the public key at line 482. Since the receiver verifies the final ECDSA signature with `pub.verify`, a forged signature can't pass. So forgery on the receiver is blocked by the final check.

Let me focus on the more subtle attacks: the MtA/OT range. The `v_theta` range check (line 291) checks each received `v_theta` in range. But what's NOT checked? Let me look at the OT receiver's `R_bits` randomness and the `delta`/`X` consistency. Let me also examine the `seed` broadcast (line 280) — `seed` and `v_theta` are sent via `plain_broadcast_and_pairwise_message`. Let me check whether the OT sender validates anything about the received `seed`/`v_theta` beyond range.

Let me look closely at the consistency check that ties partial values. The key concern: does a malicious party get to learn the honest party's `x_i`? The MtA uses Beaver-style with `a[] = {rho_i, k_i, rho_i, x_i}`. Let me check the OT protocol and whether the sender's input `D` is range-checked.

The MtA is the Gilboa multiplication with a OT-based correlation, where the consistency of the products is enforced by the ZK proofs `pi_F_eRHO_K`/`pi_F_eRHO_X` (mult proofs tying eRHO_i * eK and eRHO_i * eX) and the W/Z DH checks (line 431-432) that verify `rho*k` and `rho*x` were computed honestly. This is the standard "verifiable MtA via ElGamal-in-the-exponent" construction from the cb-mpc paper. The checks at 431-432 (`W_eRHO_K != Z_eRHO_K.R`) are the integrity check that the MtA products are consistent.

This protocol is heavily checked. Let me verify one remaining concern: the round-2 `h_consistency` check at line 94 ensures all parties agree on `(msg, Q, Qis)`. And line 73-74 validate the local key. Let me check whether `r == 0` or `s == 0` are checked, and whether `K` (the nonce point) being identity is rejected — a malicious party could try to force K to a known value. But K_i is proven via `pi_K` to be consistent with eK_i commitment. And final signature is verified.

Let me confirm the proofs verify the correct curve point membership for received commitments (preventing small-subgroup/invalid-curve). The verify functions call `curve.check`. Let me confirm `elg_com_t` arithmetic and that `eRHO_i._j`, `eK_i._j` are validated before being used in `eB` computation at line 449.

The commitment proofs are well-formed (Fischlin UC proofs, with full curve checks and range checks). The `eRHO_i._j` and `eK_i._j` used in line 449/358 are verified by `pi_eK._j.verify` (line 236) and `pi_eRHO._j.verify` (line 237) in round 4 before use. Good.

This protocol implements the full cb-mpc paper construction with consistency proofs at every round. The pass is exhaustive. Let me do one final targeted check on the `s` malleability / low-s normalization and the `r==0`/`s==0` and verify there's no missing zero-check that could be exploited — though the final `pub.verify(msg, sig)` catches any invalid signature on the receiver. Let me confirm there is no path where a non-receiver party is harmed (they don't compute anything secret-leaking in the final round; they only send `rho_k_i`/`beta`).

The honest conclusion: every received value in the signing path is bound to a verified commitment and/or covered by a ZK proof, and the receiver re-verifies the final signature. I find no exploitable missing check.

input.coverage_notes:
Deep second-pass audit of coinbase::mpc::ecdsampc::sign (src/cbmpc/protocol/ecdsa_mp.cpp, the only signing path under public APIs sign_additive/sign_ac in include/cbmpc/api/ecdsa_mp.h). Read the signing function in full (lines 37-486) plus the ZK proof verifiers in src/cbmpc/zk/zk_elgamal_com.cpp. ot_role_map is validated up front (lines 46-64) so the c_api/demo entry points cannot inject malformed maps reaching OOB.

Received-value -> validation confirmed:
- h_consistency._j (round 1->2): checked equal across parties (line 94), binding all signers to the same (msg, Q, Qis). Local key consistency also enforced: x_share*G==Qi (73) and SUM(Qis)==Q (74).
- sid_i._js / c._js: sid derived by hashing sorted pids + all sid_i (99-101); commitment c opened and verified against Ei_gen in round 2 (line 145, commitment_t.open).
- Ei_gen._j (ElGamal key DKG): commitment-opened (145) AND zk uc_dl proof pi_s verified (147) before E = SUM(E_i) is formed (150). Curve validity checked inside verify.
- ot_msg1/msg2/msg3 (PVW base-OT + extension): base.output_R does curve.check on U0/U1 (ot.cpp 89-90); OT correlation correctness is later cross-checked by the in-exponent MtA proofs (below), not trusted directly.
- v_theta._j (OT-part round 4->5): explicit range check q.is_in_range on every component (lines 289-292) before use; out-of-range rejected with E_CRYPTO.
- eK_i._j, eRHO_i._j (round 3->4): each accompanied by uc_elgamal_com proof pi_eK/pi_eRHO, verified (236-237) with full curve.check + Fischlin range checks (zk_elgamal_com.cpp 64-115) BEFORE eK/eRHO are reused (e.g. eB built at 449 from already-verified eRHO_i._j).
- eRHO_K._j, eRHO_X._j, F_eRHO_K._j, F_eRHO_X._j (round 5->6): bound by uc_elgamal_com proofs (360-361) and elgamal_com_mult proofs pi_F_* (358-359) tying F = rho * commitment; verifiers check curves and Schnorr-style equations (zk_elgamal_com.cpp 154-176). h (transcript hash) checked equal (386).
- Z_eRHO_K_i._j, Z_eRHO_X_i._j (round 6->7): uc_elgamal_com_mult_private_scalar proofs verified (388-391); h2 transcript equality checked (418).
- W_eRHO_K_i._j, W_eRHO_X_i._j, K_i._j (round 7->8): DH proofs pi_W_* and elgamal_com_pub_share_equ proof pi_K verified (419-421). Critical MtA-integrity gate: W_eRHO_K==Z_eRHO_K.R and W_eRHO_X==Z_eRHO_X.R enforced (431-432) — this is the consistency check tying the recombined rho*k / rho*x products to the committed nonce/share, blocking a malicious signer from injecting an inconsistent product.
- rho_k._j, beta._j (final all-to-one, round 8->receiver): each bound to a verified commitment via elgamal_com_pub_share_equ proofs pi_R_eRHO_K (RHO_K=rho_k_i*G vs eRHO_K) and pi_R_eB (B=beta*G vs eB) verified on the receiver (469-470). eB[j] itself is a deterministic affine combination of already-proven eRHO_X._j and eRHO_i._j.
- Final signature: receiver recomputes (r,s), low-s normalizes (478-479), and re-verifies against key.Q with pub.verify(msg,sig) (482) — any forged/biased aggregate is rejected here, and non-receiver parties send no secret-bearing value in the clear that isn't commitment-bound.

Nonce handling: k_i, rho_i are fresh bn_t::rand(q) per signer per invocation; no reuse across the protocol and no caller-influenced seed. K is committed (eK_i) and proven before r is derived, so a malicious party cannot adaptively bias the nonce after seeing others' K_i (commit-then-open ordering via the round structure and pi_K). No exploitable missing check found; result is zero findings.</coverage_notes>
</invoke>

content.text:
I completed a deep second-pass audit of the multi-party ECDSA signing path and found ZERO exploitable findings. The result and detailed coverage evidence are in the structured output.

Summary: The only in-scope signing entry is `coinbase::mpc::ecdsampc::sign` (reached via `sign_additive`/`sign_ac` in `include/cbmpc/api/ecdsa_mp.h`). I traced every received value across all 8 signing rounds and confirmed each is either (a) bound to a commitment that is later opened/verified, (b) covered by a ZK proof (uc_elgamal_com, elgamal_com_mult, elgamal_com_pub_share_equ, uc_dl, dh) that is verified with full curve and range checks before the value is consumed, or (c) explicitly range/equality checked inline. The MtA product integrity is gated by the `W == Z.R` checks (lines 431-432), nonces are freshly sampled and commit-bound before `r` is derived, and the receiver re-verifies the final ECDSA signature against the group public key. The `ot_role_map` is validated up front, closing the OOB surface. No missing required check is exploitable by a malicious signer against honest counterparties on unmodified code.
